methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Chips & Semiconductors (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 14 same-sector peers exist in Chips & Semiconductors, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 22.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
Ranovus is a Series D+ stage semiconductor company that has raised $236M to date, including a $36M strategic investment from the Canadian government (August 2025). The target operates within a fragmented Chips & Semiconductors sector comprising 14 same-sector peers: 3 public companies (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private firms including Cerebras, PsiQuantum, Etched, SambaNova Systems, and Credo Semiconductor.

Standard triangulation methods face material constraints here. Peer-median valuation cannot be applied: while 14 same-sector comparables exist, filtering by stage and valuation tier yields fewer than three datapoints, rendering sector-wide medians unreliable. Public-comp anchoring is similarly unavailable because Ranovus's revenue is not disclosed—a standard limitation for private companies—and the public semiconductor P/S median of 22.3x across the three public tickers cannot be meaningfully applied without the target's top-line figure.

The absence of applicable quantitative methods means no valuation range can be responsibly derived from the available data. Any range assignment would require either: (i) Ranovus's audited or disclosed revenue; (ii) at least three sufficiently comparable private peers with known valuations; or (iii) disclosed EBITDA or similar earnings metrics. The primary uncertainty is opacity of the target's financial performance relative to its capital raise. A credible valuation would require additional operational or financial disclosure from the company.
